items.front: T-class VOR/VORTAC/TACAN: altitude band and usable radius
items.back: 12,000 feet and below, 25 miles.

items.front: L-class VOR/VORTAC/TACAN: altitude band and usable radius
items.back: Below 18,000 feet, 40 miles.

items.front: H-class VOR/VORTAC/TACAN above FL450: usable radius distance
items.back: 100 miles (not 130 — 130 applies only 18,000 through FL450).

items.front: CL-class L/MF radio beacon: power and usable radius
items.back: Under 25 watts, 15 miles.

items.front: MH-class L/MF radio beacon: power and usable radius
items.back: Under 50 watts, 25 miles.

items.front: H-class L/MF radio beacon: power and usable radius
items.back: 50 to 1,999 watts, 50 miles.

items.front: HH-class L/MF radio beacon: power and usable radius
items.back: 2,000 watts or more, 75 miles.

items.front: TBL 4-1-3 footnote: what takes precedence when flight check differs from table?
items.back: Use the current flight check height/altitude limitations if different from the table minima.

items.front: H-class VOR: list all four altitude bands and their distances
What are the three lettered exceptions to altitude and distance limitations in 4-1-2?
items.back: (a) ATC-initiated or pilot-requested routing with radar monitoring. (b) Operational necessity with Frequency Management and Flight Inspection Offices approval. (c) Requested routing via an MTR.

items.front: GNSS exception: which suffixes and what critical qualifier?
items.back: /G, /L, /S, and /V aircraft NOT on a random impromptu route.

items.front: Exception (b): what approval is needed for operational necessity?
items.back: Approval from both the Frequency Management Office and the Flight Inspection Office.

items.front: Note 1: what separation is required for RNAV aircraft on Q routes below FL450?
items.back: Radar separation, except for GNSS-equipped /G, /L, /S, /V aircraft not on a random impromptu route.

items.front: Note 2: controller duty when clearance exceeds NAVAID limitations
items.back: Provide information and advice about significant deviations from the expected flight path, in addition to separation and airspace duties.

items.front: Does radar monitoring alone satisfy exception 4-1-2(a)?
items.back: No. It must also be ATC-initiated or pilot-requested routing. Both conditions required.

items.front: 4-1-3 core rule: crossing or holding altitude requirement
items.back: Use an altitude consistent with the limitations of the aid when clearing an aircraft to cross or hold at a fix.

items.front: Does MVA compliance satisfy the 4-1-3 crossing-altitude rule?
items.back: No. MVA ensures terrain clearance but does not guarantee the aircraft is within the NAVAID's service volume at that altitude.

items.front: VFR-on-top exception: when can a route not meeting service-volume limits be used?
items.back: Only if the aircraft requests to operate VFR-on-top on that route.

items.front: TACAN-only aircraft route definition: what NAVAIDs are used?
items.back: TACAN or VORTAC NAVAIDs, in the same manner as VOR-equipped aircraft.

items.front: TACAN-only VFR-on-top request: what is the Class A restriction?
items.back: Except in Class A airspace, a TACAN-only aircraft is expected to request VFR-on-top when insufficient TACAN or VORTAC NAVAIDs exist. In Class A, VFR-on-top is not available.

items.front: Unpublished fix divergence: formula over 30 NM up to 45 NM
items.back: Minimum divergence increases 1 degree per NM. At 40 NM: 40 degrees minimum.

items.front: Unpublished fix divergence: formula beyond 45 NM
items.back: Minimum divergence increases ½ degree per NM beyond 45 NM. 45 degrees at 45 NM, then +0.5/NM. Example: 130 NM requires 88 degrees.

items.front: Unpublished fix divergence: minimum when holding operations involved
items.back: At least 45 degrees, regardless of NAVAID distance from the fix.

items.front: Unpublished fix divergence: minimum when both NAVAIDs within 30 NM
items.back: 30 degrees (unless holding operations are involved, which requires 45 degrees).

items.front: How are MTR route description fixes classified?
items.back: Considered filed fixes. They need not be treated as unpublished fixes.

items.front: TACAN-only aircraft (M, N, P): what facilities may fixes be assigned from?
items.back: TACAN or VORTAC facilities only. They lack VOR and LF navigation capability.

items.front: TACAN-only aircraft: can they be held overhead a TACAN or VORTAC?
items.back: Never. A TACAN-only aircraft cannot be held overhead any NAVAID.

items.front: DME fix placement prohibition: where must DME fixes NOT be established?
items.back: Within the no-course signal zone of the NAVAID from which inbound holding course information would be derived.

items.front: Unpublished fix holding altitude: MEA or holding template terrain clearance?
items.back: Use the lowest assignable altitude dictated by terrain clearance for the holding pattern airspace area (template), regardless of the MEA for the route.

items.front: Unpublished fix: two definitions from 4-1-5
items.back: 1. One approved and planned for publication but not yet on charts. 2. One used in accord with fix-formation and divergence criteria.

items.front: DME vs off-route radial for unpublished fix: which is preferred?
items.back: DME must be used in lieu of off-route radials whenever possible.

items.front: Random RNAV route waypoints: what reporting status?
items.back: Automatically become compulsory reporting points for the flight unless otherwise advised by ATC.

items.front: Military turbojet single-piloted aircraft: which charts for fixes?
items.back: High altitude en route charts, high altitude instrument approach procedures charts, and SID charts, unless the pilot requests otherwise.

items.front: Info given to pilot when using an unpublished fix
items.back: The NAVAID name and, if appropriate, the radial/course/azimuth and frequency/channel.
